past_medical_history: Tuberous sclerosis complex diagnosed in childhood based on dermatologic findings
Cardiac rhabdomyoma identified on echocardiography at age 6, stable and asymptomatic
History of infantile spasms treated with vigabatrin in the first year of life, resolved by age 2
Asymptomatic hypochromic macules on trunk and lower extremities
Remote febrile seizure at age 18 months, single episode
family_history: No known family history of epilepsy
Father with hypertension and type 2 diabetes
Paternal uncle with arrhythmia of unclear type

social_history.alcohol: Rare social drinker, 1-2 drinks per month
social_history.exercise: Walks regularly, avoids swimming alone due to seizure history

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert and oriented x4. Speech fluent with intact naming and repetition. Able to recall 2/3 objects at 5 minutes. Mild word-finding difficulty noted during spontaneous conversation, though formal confrontation naming was intact. Affect mildly anxious but appropriate.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: Pupils 3 mm, equal and reactive. Visual fields full to confrontation. Extraocular movements intact without nystagmus. Facial sensation and motor function symmetric. Hearing grossly intact bilaterally. Palate elevates symmetrically. Sternocleidomastoid and trapezius strength 5/5. Tongue midline.
neurological_exam.motor: Bulk and tone normal throughout. Strength 5/5 in all four extremities proximally and distally. No drift.
neurological_exam.sensory: Light touch, pinprick, vibration, and proprioception intact throughout. No extinction to double simultaneous stimulation.
neurological_exam.reflexes: 2+ and symmetric at biceps, triceps, brachioradialis, patellar, and Achilles. Plantar responses flexor bilaterally.
neurological_exam.coordination: Finger-to-nose and heel-to-shin testing intact bilaterally without dysmetria. No tremor.
neurological_exam.gait: Steady, narrow-based gait. Tandem walk intact. Romberg negative.
neurological_exam.additional: Dermatologic examination notable for three hypomelanotic macules (ash-leaf spots) on the upper back and one on the left anterior thigh, each approximately 2-4 cm. Small fibrous plaque on the forehead consistent with fibrous cephalic plaque. No facial angiofibromas.

patient.chief_complaint: Recurrent episodes of staring and confusion with involuntary mouth and hand movements over the past 3 months, increasing in frequency.
patient.history_present_illness: A 29-year-old right-handed woman with a known history of tuberous sclerosis complex and remote infantile spasms presents to the neurology service after being admitted through the emergency department following a witnessed generalized convulsion at her university laboratory. She reports a 3-month history of recurrent stereotyped episodes beginning with an indescribable 'rising sensation' in her stomach followed by an altered sense of awareness during which she appears to stare blankly and perform repetitive lip-smacking and fumbling movements with her right hand. These episodes last approximately 60 to 90 seconds, after which she experiences confusion and mild headache lasting 15 to 30 minutes. She initially attributed the episodes to anxiety and panic attacks, especially given the stress of her graduate studies. Over the past three weeks, the frequency has increased from once weekly to nearly daily, and yesterday's episode progressed to bilateral tonic-clonic convulsions witnessed by her laboratory supervisor, who called emergency services. She was found to be confused and somnolent on arrival, with a tongue bite on the left lateral margin. She denies any recent illness, fever, or new medications. She has been compliant with her valproic acid and clobazam but acknowledges occasionally missing doses over the past month due to her busy schedule. She had been seizure-free since age 14 and had considered discontinuing her antiepileptic medications with her previous neurologist two years ago but ultimately decided to continue them. She reports no recent head trauma, no changes in sleep pattern beyond occasionally staying late in the lab, and no exposure to flashing lights or other known seizure triggers. An electrocardiographic abnormality was incidentally noted on her admission ECG, prompting additional cardiac workup.

background.posterior_dominant_rhythm: 8.5 Hz alpha rhythm, symmetric, reactive to eye opening
background.symmetry: Grossly symmetric with subtle amplitude asymmetry, left temporal region slightly lower
background.sleep_architecture: Stage II sleep captured, with vertex waves and sleep spindles

findings.morphology: Diphasic sharp waves with a broad field, followed by slow wave; morphology suggestive of epileptiform activity but somewhat atypical

findings.frequency: Intermittent, seen in brief runs lasting 2-5 seconds

findings.morphology: Arciform 6-11 Hz wicket rhythm, monophasic, occurring in brief trains

activating_procedures.hyperventilation: No significant change. No epileptiform discharges activated.
activating_procedures.photic_stimulation: Symmetric photic driving response at 12-18 Hz. No photoparoxysmal response.

eeg.impression: Abnormal EEG due to rare left anterior temporal sharp transients and intermittent left temporal slowing. Wicket spikes noted as a benign variant and should not be confused with pathological epileptiform activity. Clinical correlation recommended.
eeg.limitations: 30-minute routine recording; interictal sensitivity is limited. No clinical events were captured during the recording.

findings.location: Left mesial temporal lobe, involving the anterior hippocampus and parahippocampal gyrus

signal_characteristics.T1: Iso- to hypointense relative to cortex
signal_characteristics.FLAIR: Hyperintense with blurring of gray-white junction

findings.location: Along the wall of the left lateral ventricle at the level of the caudate head
findings.mass_effect: None. Does not obstruct the foramen of Monro.

signal_characteristics.FLAIR: Subtle signal increase, could represent cortical tuber extension vs early sclerotic change vs artifact

findings.location: Right frontal lobe, superior frontal gyrus

additional_observations: No evidence of subependymal giant cell astrocytoma (SEGA); subependymal nodule is stable-appearing and non-enhancing
Mild non-specific scattered white matter T2 hyperintensities in bilateral periventricular regions, likely related to TSC
mri.impression: The left mesial temporal cortical tuber is the most clinically relevant lesion given the patient's seizure semiology. There is subtle hippocampal asymmetry with borderline volumetric reduction on the left, though this is below the threshold for definitive hippocampal sclerosis. The right frontal tuber is an additional potential epileptogenic focus but is less likely to account for the temporal lobe semiology. Clinical correlation recommended.

findings: Pronounced dome-shaped ST-segment elevation in leads V2 and V3, approximately 2 mm
No reciprocal ST depression in other leads
Normal R-wave progression across precordial leads
ecg.interpretation: Sinus rhythm at 78 bpm with localized ST-segment elevation in V2-V3. The dome-shaped morphology and absence of reciprocal changes or PR depression make acute pericarditis and acute coronary syndrome less likely. The pattern is atypical for early repolarization.

labs.interpretation: Abnormal values: TSH 4.8 mIU/L (H), Valproic acid level 38 mcg/mL (L), Prolactin 48.2 ng/mL (H), Lactic acid 2.8 mmol/L (H).
abnormal_values_summary: Lactic acid 2.8 mmol/L (mildly elevated, reference 0.5-2.2, post-ictal)

background.sleep_architecture: Normal sleep architecture with vertex waves, K-complexes, and spindles

findings.location: Left anterior temporal (F7/T3), maximum at T3
findings.frequency: Frequent, approximately 15-20 per hour during drowsiness and sleep
findings.morphology: Sharp waves with consistent diphasic morphology and aftergoing slow wave, field restricted to left temporal chain

findings.location: Left temporal onset (F7/T3), with secondary spread to left parasagittal and then bilateral
findings.frequency: One clinical event captured on Day 2 of monitoring
findings.morphology: Rhythmic 5-7 Hz theta evolving to sustained rhythmic delta over left temporal region, duration 72 seconds. Terminated with post-ictal left temporal slowing and attenuation.

output.impression: Abnormal prolonged video-EEG monitoring capturing one habitual clinical seizure with unequivocal left temporal onset, plus frequent left anterior temporal interictal epileptiform discharges. Seizure semiology (epigastric aura, behavioral arrest, oral and manual automatisms) is classic for mesial temporal lobe onset. Clinical correlation recommended.
output.limitations: 3-day monitoring period. Only one seizure captured.

findings.location: Left mesial temporal lobe involving anterior hippocampus and parahippocampal gyrus

signal_characteristics.FLAIR: Hyperintense, well-delineated on thin coronal cuts

findings.borders: Better delineated on 3T epilepsy protocol; extends into the subiculum

findings.location: Left hippocampus, body and tail segments

signal_characteristics.FLAIR: Definite hyperintensity of the left hippocampal body, not attributable to artifact on high-resolution coronal imaging

volumetrics.left_hippocampus: 2.21 cm³ (5th percentile for age/sex, significantly reduced)

additional_observations: Thin coronal FLAIR cuts confirm the left hippocampal signal abnormality is real and not artifact
Subependymal nodule along left lateral ventricle unchanged, non-enhancing
output.impression: This dual pathology is a well-described finding in tuberous sclerosis complex and strongly supports the left mesial temporal lobe as the epileptogenic zone. No evidence of SEGA transformation. Clinical correlation recommended.

findings.location: Left temporal lobe — mesial and lateral temporal cortex

signal_characteristics.FDG_uptake: Markedly reduced compared to contralateral right temporal lobe and ipsilateral frontal cortex

findings.borders: Corresponds to the region of the cortical tuber and extends slightly beyond

signal_characteristics.FDG_uptake: Normal to mildly reduced, not clearly lateralizing

additional_observations: Remainder of cortex shows symmetric and normal FDG uptake
output.impression: FDG-PET demonstrates significant left temporal hypometabolism concordant with the EEG and MRI findings, strongly lateralizing the epileptogenic zone to the left temporal lobe. The right frontal tuber region shows only mildly reduced uptake, unlikely to be a significant independent epileptogenic focus. Clinical correlation recommended.

output.interpretation: Abnormal values: Verbal Memory (CVLT-II total) 38 T-score (H), Verbal Memory Delayed Recall 34 T-score (H), Semantic Fluency (Animals) 36 T-score (H).

output.query: tuberous sclerosis complex temporal lobe epilepsy surgical management

results.title: Epilepsy surgery in tuberous sclerosis complex: a systematic review
results.summary: Epilepsy surgery achieves seizure freedom (Engel class I) in 57-75% of TSC patients with a single dominant focus. Dual pathology (tuber + hippocampal sclerosis) is common and may require more extensive resection.

results.title: mTOR inhibitors in epilepsy: rationale and clinical evidence
results.summary: Everolimus (mTOR inhibitor) has shown efficacy in reducing seizure frequency in TSC-associated epilepsy. The EXIST-3 trial demonstrated a ≥50% seizure reduction in 40% of patients. Considered for drug-resistant cases, particularly when multiple tubers preclude focal resection.

results.title: Vigabatrin as first-line treatment for infantile spasms in TSC
results.summary: Vigabatrin remains first-line for infantile spasms in TSC. Long-term visual field defects are a concern. Adult TSC epilepsy management should follow standard focal epilepsy algorithms with consideration of mTOR pathway targeted therapy.

output.summary: In TSC-associated temporal lobe epilepsy, management follows a stepwise approach: optimize AED therapy first (standard focal epilepsy drugs), consider mTOR inhibitors (everolimus) for refractory cases with multiple tubers, and evaluate for epilepsy surgery when a single dominant focus is identified. Presurgical evaluation (video-EEG, high-resolution MRI, PET, neuropsychological testing) is critical for surgical candidacy. Dual pathology (tuber + hippocampal sclerosis) is well-described and may affect surgical planning.

output.proposed: Valproic acid dose increase or addition of lamotrigine
interactions: Valproic acid inhibits lamotrigine metabolism (UGT1A4 inhibition), increasing lamotrigine levels 2-fold. Lamotrigine must be started at half the usual dose (12.5 mg/day) and titrated slowly when combined with valproic acid.

Valproic acid + combined oral contraceptive: oral contraceptives can reduce valproic acid levels by 10-20% via UGT induction. Conversely, valproic acid does NOT significantly reduce oral contraceptive efficacy (unlike enzyme-inducing AEDs).
Clobazam + valproic acid: mild pharmacokinetic interaction; valproic acid may slightly increase clobazam levels. Generally well-tolerated.
contraindications: Valproic acid is contraindicated in pregnancy (Category X) — teratogenic risk. Patient is on oral contraceptive; ensure reliable contraception if continuing valproic acid.
Do NOT combine valproic acid with carbapenems (e.g., meropenem) — rapid and dramatic reduction in valproic acid levels.
warnings: Monitor ammonia levels with valproic acid dose increase, especially if hepatic symptoms develop
Weight gain is common with valproic acid; BMI should be monitored
Consider switching from valproic acid to lamotrigine or levetiracetam if pregnancy is planned in the future
output.formulary_status: Both valproic acid and lamotrigine are on formulary
alternatives: Levetiracetam — no significant drug interactions, weight-neutral, safe in pregnancy
Lacosamide — well-tolerated adjunctive option for focal epilepsy
Oxcarbazepine — effective for focal epilepsy but interacts with oral contraceptives (enzyme induction)
Everolimus — disease-specific option for TSC-associated epilepsy

findings: Transthoracic echocardiography performed: single hyperechogenic, well-circumscribed nodule measuring 0.4 x 0.3 cm in the mid interventricular septum, right-facing
Normal left ventricular size and function (EF 62%)
No inflow or outflow tract obstruction
output.interpretation: The tumor is small, non-obstructive, and hemodynamically insignificant. This lesion accounts for the localized ST-segment elevation seen on the surface ECG. Left ventricular function is normal. No intervention required; continued periodic surveillance is appropriate.

ground_truth.primary_diagnosis: Focal epilepsy — temporal lobe, secondary to tuberous sclerosis complex with left mesial temporal cortical tuber and dual pathology (hippocampal sclerosis)

differential.diagnosis: Focal epilepsy due to cortical tuber alone (TSC)
differential.key_distinguishing: MRI shows cortical tuber in left mesial temporal lobe; EEG localizes to left temporal region. Dual pathology with hippocampal sclerosis is additive.
Elevated post-ictal prolactin, witnessed tongue bite, stereotyped semiology with EEG correlate all argue against PNES.
ECG abnormality is incidental and due to known rhabdomyoma. Normal cardiac biomarkers, normal echocardiogram, and clear epileptiform EEG changes exclude cardiac syncope.
Subtherapeutic valproic acid level is a precipitating factor, not the underlying etiology. Structural lesion is the primary cause.

differential.diagnosis: Frontal lobe epilepsy (from right frontal tuber)
differential.key_distinguishing: Seizure semiology (epigastric aura, oral automatisms) is classic for temporal onset, not frontal. EEG and PET localize to left temporal, not right frontal.

optimal_actions.action: Obtain routine EEG to evaluate for interictal epileptiform activity
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Left anterior temporal sharp transients and intermittent temporal slowing

optimal_actions.action: Order brain MRI to identify structural epileptogenic lesion
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Left mesial temporal cortical tuber, hippocampal asymmetry, and additional TSC features

optimal_actions.action: Obtain comprehensive labs including AED levels, metabolic panel, and post-ictal markers
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Subtherapeutic valproic acid level, elevated post-ictal prolactin, normal electrolytes

optimal_actions.action: Review ECG abnormality in context of cardiac history
optimal_actions.expected_finding: ST elevation in V2-V3 attributed to known cardiac rhabdomyoma, not acute cardiac pathology

optimal_actions.action: Request prolonged video-EEG monitoring to capture habitual seizure and confirm localization
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Left temporal seizure onset with classic mesial temporal semiology

optimal_actions.action: Request 3T MRI with epilepsy protocol for hippocampal characterization
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Confirmed dual pathology: cortical tuber + significant hippocampal sclerosis

optimal_actions.action: Check drug interactions for AED optimization
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Valproic acid-lamotrigine interaction, contraception considerations, pregnancy risk

optimal_actions.action: Search literature for TSC-associated epilepsy management guidelines
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Evidence supporting AED optimization, mTOR inhibitors, and surgical evaluation for refractory cases

critical_actions: Obtain EEG to identify epileptiform focus
Obtain brain MRI to identify structural lesion (cortical tuber)
Check antiepileptic drug levels to identify subtherapeutic valproic acid as a precipitating factor
Correctly attribute ECG abnormality to known cardiac rhabdomyoma rather than pursuing unnecessary acute cardiac workup
Recognize the diagnosis of focal temporal lobe epilepsy in the context of tuberous sclerosis complex
Recommend optimization of antiepileptic therapy as the immediate management priority
contraindicated_actions: Initiating anticoagulation or thrombolysis based on ECG changes (no acute coronary syndrome)
Performing lumbar puncture without clear indication (no signs of infection or inflammation)
Abruptly discontinuing valproic acid (risk of seizure exacerbation or status epilepticus)
Prescribing carbamazepine or oxcarbazepine without addressing contraceptive interaction
Ordering urgent cardiac catheterization for the ST-segment elevation (known rhabdomyoma)
key_reasoning_points: The ECG abnormality (ST elevation in V2-V3) is a red herring attributable to the known cardiac rhabdomyoma in the interventricular septum, not an acute cardiac event — the dome-shaped morphology, absence of reciprocal changes, normal cardiac biomarkers, and patient history all support this
Subtherapeutic valproic acid level is a precipitating factor for seizure recurrence in a patient with an established structural epileptogenic lesion, not a new diagnosis

The presence of tuberous sclerosis complex (ash-leaf spots, cortical tubers, subependymal nodule, cardiac rhabdomyoma) provides the unifying diagnosis explaining both the neurological and cardiac findings
EEG findings require careful interpretation — wicket spikes are a benign variant and must not be conflated with pathological epileptiform discharges
Multiple cortical tubers (left temporal and right frontal) necessitate careful localization of the epileptogenic zone; seizure semiology and EEG must guide which tuber is epileptogenic
The mildly elevated TSH is an incidental finding (subclinical hypothyroidism) that should not distract from the epilepsy diagnosis but should be monitored
Dual pathology (cortical tuber + hippocampal sclerosis) is a recognized phenomenon in TSC and affects surgical planning

metadata.difficulty_description: Moderate difficulty due to: (1) an incidental ECG abnormality (ST elevation from cardiac rhabdomyoma) that could misdirect the agent toward a cardiac workup, (2) subtle and equivocal initial EEG findings requiring follow-up monitoring, (3) an incidental mildly elevated TSH, (4) multiple cortical tubers requiring determination of which is the epileptogenic focus, and (5) the need to recognize subtherapeutic AED levels as a precipitating factor rather than a new diagnosis.
